questions.stem: A patient with severe preeclampsia at 34 weeks develops an eclamptic seizure. She is on magnesium sulfate. Which intervention takes priority during the active seizure?

options.text: Administer IV diazepam 5 mg to stop the seizure
Insert a padded tongue blade to protect the airway
Position on her left side, protect from injury, administer oxygen, and note seizure duration
Give a magnesium sulfate bolus of 4 g IV immediately

questions.rationale: During an eclamptic seizure: protect the patient from injury, position on left lateral decubitus (reduces aortocaval compression, improves uteroplacental blood flow), apply oxygen via face mask, and time the seizure. IV diazepam (A) is not first-line; magnesium is the drug of choice for eclampsia treatment and prevention of recurrent seizures. A padded tongue blade (B) should never be inserted during an active seizure — high risk of injury to teeth and nurse. A magnesium bolus (D) may be given post-seizure if patient is not already at therapeutic levels, but protecting the patient during the active seizure comes first.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Administer IV diazepam 5 mg to stop the seizure" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.b: "Insert a padded tongue blade to protect the airway" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Eclamptic seizure priority: protect from injury, left lateral position, O2, time seizure — never insert anything into the" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Give a magnesium sulfate bolus of 4 g IV immediately" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Eclamptic seizure priority: protect from injury, left lateral position, O2, time seizure — never insert anything into the" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Eclamptic seizure priority: protect from injury, left lateral position, O2, time seizure — never insert anything into the mouth during a seizure.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old on haloperidol develops sudden onset torticollis (involuntary neck twisting), oculogyric crisis (eyes fixed upward), and severe muscle rigidity within 3 days of starting the medication. Which type of extrapyramidal symptom (EPS) is this, and what is the antidote?

options.text: Tardive dyskinesia; no effective antidote, may require medication discontinuation
Akathisia; treat with propranolol or benzodiazepines
Acute dystonia; treat with IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine
Pseudoparkinsonism; treat with amantadine or reduce antipsychotic dose

questions.rationale: Acute dystonia presents within hours to days of starting a high-potency antipsychotic (like haloperidol) as sudden, painful muscle spasms — torticollis, oculogyric crisis, opisthotonus. Treatment is IV or IM diphenhydramine (Benadryl) or benztropine (Cogentin), which are anticholinergic agents. Tardive dyskinesia (A) develops after months-years of use as involuntary repetitive movements (lip smacking, tongue protrusion). Akathisia (B) is inner restlessness and urge to move. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) mimics Parkinson's disease with tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia.

distractorRationales.a: The cue "severe muscle rigidity within 3 days of starting the medication" suggests medication toxicity, so "Tardive dyskinesia; no effective antidote, may require medication discontinuation" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.b: "Akathisia; treat with propranolol or benzodiazepines" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Pseudoparkinsonism (D) mimics Parkinson's disease with tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: "Pseudoparkinsonism; treat with amantadine or reduce antipsychotic dose" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Pseudoparkinsonism (D) mimics Parkinson's disease with tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.

questions.takeaway: Acute dystonia (torticollis, oculogyric crisis) within days of antipsychotic = IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine (anticholinergics).

questions.stem: A 12-year-old presents with 18 hours of periumbilical pain now localized to the right lower quadrant, nausea, fever of 39.1°C, and rebound tenderness. The nurse assesses guarding and rigidity of the abdomen on palpation. Which finding most suggests appendiceal perforation has occurred?

options.text: Rebound tenderness in the RLQ
Migration of pain from periumbilical to RLQ over 24 hours
Sudden relief of pain followed by diffuse abdominal rigidity and fever spike
Nausea and anorexia with low-grade fever

questions.rationale: The classic sign of appendiceal perforation is sudden relief of pain when the appendix ruptures (release of pressure), immediately followed by diffuse peritonitis as fecal material spreads throughout the peritoneum: diffuse board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness throughout the abdomen, and temperature spike. WBC may dramatically rise. The child may briefly appear to improve (pain relief) before rapidly deteriorating. Pre-perforation signs: McBurney's point tenderness, RLQ rebound tenderness, Rovsing's sign, psoas sign. Post-perforation: diffuse guarding, paralytic ileus. Emergency appendectomy is required.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Sudden relief of pain followed by diffuse abdominal rigidity and fever spike" fits the safer response to the cue "A 12-year-old presents with 18 hours of periumbilical pain now localized to the right lower quadrant, nausea, fever".
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Sudden relief of pain followed by diffuse abdominal rigidity and fever spike" fits the safer response to the cue "The nurse assesses guarding and rigidity of the abdomen on palpation".
distractorRationales.d: "Nausea and anorexia with low-grade fever" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Appendiceal perforation: sudden pain relief → followed by diffuse board-like rigidity + diffuse rebound + fever spike =" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Appendiceal perforation: sudden pain relief → followed by diffuse board-like rigidity + diffuse rebound + fever spike = peritonitis from perforation; emergency appendectomy required.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Appendiceal perforation: sudden pain relief → followed by diffuse board-like rigidity + diffuse rebound + fever spike = peritonitis from perforation; emergency appendectomy required.
visualRationale.conclusion: Appendiceal perforation: sudden pain relief → followed by diffuse board-like rigidity + diffuse rebound + fever spike = peritonitis from perforation; emergency appendectomy required.

questions.stem: A patient is 6 hours post open abdominal surgery and reports pain rated 8/10. The patient is on scheduled ketorolac IV and a hydromorphone PCA but frequently presses the PCA demand button. Respiratory rate is 14 and SpO2 is 97%. Which is the most appropriate nursing response?

options.text: Contact the physician to increase the PCA demand dose — the patient clearly has inadequate pain control
Withhold all PCA doses and reassess in 2 hours — respiratory rate below 16 requires opioid restriction
Reassess pain using a validated scale, review PCA usage data and lockout history, maximize non-opioid adjuncts (IV acetaminophen if not given), and contact physician if pain remains uncontrolled
Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV prophylactically to protect respiratory reserve before adjusting the PCA

questions.rationale: Effective post-operative pain management requires a systematic, multimodal approach. Steps: (1) Reassess pain thoroughly — PQRST (location, quality, timing — is this expected surgical pain or a new complication like wound dehiscence?); (2) Review PCA data — are demand attempts successful or being locked out? Is the patient pressing correctly? Is the demand dose reaching the lockout ceiling?; (3) Ensure non-opioid adjuncts are maximized: scheduled acetaminophen (reduces opioid requirements 20-30%), ketorolac, and consider regional analgesia; (4) Contact physician if pain remains uncontrolled despite optimized non-opioids. SpO2 97% + RR 14 indicates adequate ventilation — opioids are NOT contraindicated. The outdated practice of withholding opioids for RR <16 without clinical signs of respiratory depression is not evidence-based and results in undertreated pain. Prophylactic naloxone reverses analgesia and is inappropriate without signs of opioid toxicity.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Respiratory rate is 14 and SpO2 is 97%" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Withhold all PCA doses and reassess in 2 hours — respiratory rate below 16 requires opioid restriction" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it relies on notification without the bedside safety step. "Reassess pain using a validated scale, review PCA usage data and lockout history, maximize non-opioid adjuncts" fits the safer response to the cue "SpO2 97% + RR 14 indicates adequate ventilation — opioids are NOT contraindicated".

questions.takeaway: Post-op pain: systematic multimodal assessment — maximize non-opioids (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), review PCA data, then escalate to MD. RR 14 + SpO2 97% with no signs of toxicity does NOT justify withholding opioids.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40" moves care toward routine care before patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season" fits the safer response to the cue "A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention".
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps" would not correct the active problem. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%" fits the safer response to the cue "Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority".

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic" fits the safer response to the cue "Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension".
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic" fits the safer response to the cue "Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension".
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: "Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube" misses the bedside risk. The cue "medication administration remain with the RN" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care" moves care toward teaching or reassurance before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: "Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "Unilateral calf pain, warmth" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: "The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring)" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "The SpO2 is 91%" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
